title: What Song Comes to Mind?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The letter quotes contemporary reporting and a detailed *Junior Vikatan* assessment; their claims remain attributed. The Rule 110 total is transcribed exactly as visibly printed here—Rs 1,72,196, without a larger unit—even though related letters give a crore-denominated figure. This apparent source-level omission is not silently repaired. The Tamil reading copy below corrects only unambiguous OCR defects, while the canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: For the past five years, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa has completely disregarded the welfare of Tamil Nadu's people, displayed arrogance and indifference toward every problem, acted rashly, and ruined the administration. Yet at her first election campaign meeting, she spoke about the achievements of AIADMK rule in a way that left listeners open-mouthed, and congratulated herself.
Chief Minister Jayalalithaa! Please descend for a moment from the heavens in which you perpetually travel. Look calmly and directly at what is happening in the country, and understand it.

Consider this report in the Tamil edition of *The Hindu* dated 10 April 2016. Its headline reads: “Youth's Murder, Farmer's Suicide and Other Incidents: Six Notices to Tamil Nadu Government in One Month—Human Rights Commission Acts.”

The detailed report said that, in the previous month alone, the National Human Rights Commission sent the Tamil Nadu Government notices seeking explanations concerning six separate incidents, and it described each incident. If the Human Rights Commission itself sent this Government six notices in a single month, does that not reveal the renown of AIADMK rule? Apart from these, the courts have heaped countless and continuing condemnations upon the AIADMK Government.

If the Human Rights Commission sent six notices, the High Court has sent this Government innumerable notices. And if one examined the list of fines the High Court has imposed upon senior government officials, Jayalalithaa's administrative ability would shine brilliantly into view.

At her Chennai election campaign meeting, Jayalalithaa thundered that all fifty-four schemes announced in the election manifesto had been implemented. During the past five years, however, she announced with great fanfare 187 schemes in the Legislative Assembly under Rule 110, worth Rs 1,72,196. Why did she not open her mouth to say how many of those announcements had been implemented? Why has the Government refused repeated opposition demands for a white paper on them?

Set aside for a moment whether the people of Tamil Nadu are satisfied with Jayalalithaa's Government and its actions. Are even members of her own party satisfied? How many former and serving members of her Cabinet say what things behind her back? Those still holding office may praise her and make her happy to protect their positions, but do all those who served as legislators praise Jayalalithaa sincerely?

Newspapers have reported what the lawyers who appeared for Jayalalithaa in the disproportionate-assets case are saying. Many lawyers worked night and day on that case, forgetting their children and families, yet many of them were reportedly denied an opportunity to contest this general election.

They are said to have expected Jayalalithaa to summon and commend all those lawyers after she was acquitted, but she did not even do that. They reportedly regret that people such as Navaneethakrishnan, who appeared in the case only for a short time, were given posts such as Advocate General, Chairman of the Tamil Nadu Public Service Commission and Rajya Sabha MP.

In earlier days, people would say, “You repeat the same thing again and again like a scratched HMV gramophone record.” Chief Minister Jayalalithaa rarely opens her mouth. Whenever she does, without knowing what is happening outside, she never fails to repeat what she has said many times before: “Tamil Nadu has become a power-surplus State; uninterrupted electricity is being supplied.”
But what is the actual position? The answer appears in *Dinamalar* itself, dated 10 April 2016. Under the headline “Unannounced Power Cuts: Suffering without Sleep or Water,” it reported:

> “Residents are facing various difficulties because of unannounced power cuts in Adyar, Velachery and surrounding areas. During the past several days, unannounced cuts have occurred every day, both during the day and at night, in Adyar, Besant Nagar, Thiruvanmiyur, Taramani, Velachery and Guindy. Electricity Board officials say the cuts result from cable damage during road work and transformer installation. But power cuts occur even in places where no work is under way.”
The report demonstrates how false Jayalalithaa's claim is.
Like the single grain of rice used to test a whole pot, the newspaper report is only one example of power cuts occurring every day in many parts of the State.

Do you know what promise Jayalalithaa made to the people before the 2011 election? “I will eliminate power cuts within three months,” she said. Not three months, not thirty months, but sixty months have passed. The power cuts have neither departed nor died. During these five years, students suffered because of them. Micro and small industries suffered. Large factories fled in alarm rather than come to Tamil Nadu and begin operations.

Jayalalithaa spoke yesterday as though she had forgotten that micro and small industries in the Ambattur Industrial Estate and Coimbatore region were severely weakened and that industrial units in the estates pulled down their shutters. Did she speak after forgetting, or did she deliberately conceal the truth?

The people of Tamil Nadu already know that Jayalalithaa never says anything because she intends to do it. As she campaigns, let Chief Minister Jayalalithaa stop opening and pouring out sacks of lies. If she has any truth at hand, let her speak about it. Otherwise, one feels like singing: “Born in falsehood and raised in falsehood—Chief Minister Jayalalithaa!”

On page two today, 11 April 2016, *Dinamalar* carried a half-page report under the headline “Tamil Nadu Electricity Board under Investigation—Black Money Concealed through Coal Imports.” If the Chief Minister merely read reports like these, she herself could understand how far the material written for her departs from the truth.
At the very beginning of her speech, Jayalalithaa said that every scheme announced in the AIADMK's 2011 election manifesto had been implemented.
Let me recall just a few items from the AIADMK's 2011 Assembly election manifesto:

* Three-phase electricity connections will be provided to every village and town within four years, ensuring an uninterrupted power supply. * An “Electricity Protection Force” staffed by retired military personnel will be formed to prevent electricity theft. * By 2012, within two years, 1,000 megawatts of electricity will be generated from municipal waste in 151 municipalities and corporations. * By 2013, ten 300-megawatt solar-energy parks will be established, generating 3,000 megawatts of electricity. * Steps will be taken to increase wind power to thirty per cent and nuclear power to twenty-five per cent. * Steps will be taken to generate electricity from 200-kilowatt biomass biogas facilities in 160 village panchayats, creating employment for 64,000 people in rural areas. * Solar electricity will be provided to homes below the poverty line. * Steps will be taken to operate rural streetlights using solar power.
These are the promises concerning electricity alone in the AIADMK election manifesto.

After letting every one of these promises fly rapidly away on the wind, she has spent five years repeating the lie of a “power-surplus State” and “elimination of power cuts.” Will merely chanting this incantation make a mango fall?

At her first election campaign meeting, Jayalalithaa thundered, “We have implemented everything stated in the election manifesto.” In the Legislative Assembly, she raised her voice even higher and declared, “We have not only fulfilled the promises we made during the 2011 Assembly election; we have implemented many additional schemes for the people of Tamil Nadu.”

The answer came not from the DMK but from *Junior Vikatan*. In its issue of 7 February 2016, journalist S.A.M. Barakath Ali wrote a detailed three-page article titled “Five Years of Rule... Election Promises... Has the Chief Minister Fulfilled Them?”
Under the heading “Promises That Fizzled Out,” it listed:

> “A coastal highway from Chennai to Kanniyakumari; an ‘Aero Park’ in southern Tamil Nadu; prevention of online trading; a scientific waste-disposal facility to treat Tiruppur dye effluent; free bus passes for those over fifty-eight; every police station under electronic governance; six garment special economic zones with farmers as shareholders; special instruction in schools to learn other languages alongside the mother tongue; Tamil in the courts; preference in government employment for those educated in Tamil; steps to prevent wild animals from entering residential areas and agricultural land; doubling information-technology development; and a mobile electronic-governance programme.”
The article displayed these in a boxed item.
At its opening, the article stated:

> “Jayalalithaa's assertion that every promise in the AIADMK election manifesto has been fulfilled is merely empty bravado. Ninety per cent of the promises have gone flat. The coloured fireworks set alight during the election have fizzled out.”
I shall mention only a few passages from that article. *Junior Vikatan* reproduced the wording of the AIADMK election manifesto and then described what became of each promise.

> “‘Uninterrupted electricity to bring light to a darkened Tamil Nadu. A special programme will be implemented for this purpose,’ the AIADMK announced in its manifesto. Only the rulers know when that special programme will see the light of day. Promises of ‘three-phase electricity within four years, an Electricity Protection Force of former military personnel to suppress electricity theft, and prevention of electricity misuse’ are all hanging in shreds. > > “‘Cable television will be provided at a low rate with a government subsidy,’ they said. Cable television for a monthly charge of seventy rupees was supposedly implemented. But no one knows where only seventy rupees is being collected. Jayalalithaa, who says, ‘I am because of the people; I am for the people,’ must tell us when the Fishermen Protection Force, thirteen refrigerated fish parks, and a deep-sea fish-processing and export ship park will arrive. > > “‘The automobile, telecommunications, electronics, construction and shipbuilding sectors will be encouraged and developed in the southern, eastern and western regions of Tamil Nadu; steps will be taken to attract Rs 10,000 crore in foreign investment into shipbuilding; and special economic zones will be created by turning farmers who provide land into shareholders.’ What became of these promises to industry? > > “First supporting the Central Government's Land Acquisition Act and then retreating, and conducting an investors' conference at the very end of the Government's term—these are samples of the concern shown for industry. > > “They said law and order would be maintained without fear or favour. The 9,948 murders and one lakh robberies during five years are the good-conduct certificate awarded to law and order. > > “The bomb attack at Minister Sellur Raju's office, the Ramajayam murder case, caste violence in Dharmapuri and Villupuram, and the Paramakudi police firing made law and order a laughing stock. > > “‘The problems of the film industry will be resolved’ was another promise. For films such as *Vishwaroopam*, *Thuppakki* and *Thalaivaa* even to reach theatres, did they not have to knock at the doors of the Secretariat? Grants for quality films, small-screen awards, and the film-industry luminary awards presented in the names of Anna, M.G.R., Kannadasan and Sivaji were all tied up and put away.”

The *Junior Vikatan* article continues at length with many more details. Yet in this state of affairs, Chief Minister Jayalalithaa thundered at her very first campaign meeting that every scheme in the election manifesto had been implemented.

I have cited only a list of some manifesto schemes, and even for these I searched out what magazines such as *Junior Vikatan* wrote a few weeks ago. Had Chief Minister Jayalalithaa read that issue, she would not have voiced this claim at her election campaign meeting; she would not have dared to tell these lies.

At her Chennai election campaign meeting, Jayalalithaa thundered that all fifty-four schemes announced in the election manifesto had been implemented. During the past five years, however, she announced with great fanfare 187 schemes in the Legislative Assembly under Rule 110, worth Rs 1,72,196. Why did she not open her mouth to say how many of those announcements had been implemented?

Why has the Government refused repeated opposition demands for a white paper on them? Jayalalithaa did not fulfil the promises in the election manifesto. Nor did she implement the schemes announced under Rule 110 in the Legislative Assembly. She does not even care that they remain unimplemented.

The people of Tamil Nadu already know that Jayalalithaa never says anything because she intends to do it. As she campaigns, let Chief Minister Jayalalithaa stop opening and pouring out sacks of lies. If she has any truth at hand, let her speak about it. Otherwise, one feels like singing: “Born in falsehood and raised in falsehood—Chief Minister Jayalalithaa!”
With affection,
M.K.
